children.name: Cettia
children.description: Cetti's warbler and its Asian relatives — skulking, rounded passerines of dense undergrowth. Cetti's Warbler is renowned for its explosive, loud song that seems impossible for such a small, secretive bird. The genus is named after Italian zoologist Francesco Cetti.

children.name: Cettia cetti
children.description: A stocky, chestnut-brown warbler with a rounded tail and a pale supercilium. Despite its skulking nature, its song is one of the loudest and most startling of any European bird — a sudden burst of ringing notes that erupts from dense waterside vegetation. Expanding northwards in Europe; increasingly recorded in southern Sweden. Resident year-round.

children.name: Cettia brunnea
children.description: A small bush warbler found from the Himalayas through southern China and Southeast Asia. Warm brown above, pale below with brown flanks. Skulks in thick undergrowth, often near water. Its song is a series of clear, descending whistles.

children.name: Cettia castaneocoronata
children.description: A tiny, brightly coloured warbler of Himalayan and Southeast Asian montane forests with a vivid chestnut crown and nape, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. It skulks in dense undergrowth, constantly flicking its wings, and its high-pitched song is often the only clue to its presence. It is more terrestrial than most cettias, hopping mouse-like through the leaf litter.

children.name: Cisticola
children.description: Cisticolas — a large genus of small, streaky warblers found across Africa, Asia, and southern Europe. They are named for their characteristic 'cisticola' buzzing or trilling songs, often given in aerial displays above grassland. Over 50 species, many of which are notoriously difficult to identify in the field.

children.name: Cisticola juncidis
children.description: A tiny, streaky brown bird of wet grasslands and rice paddies. Its name comes from its song — a monotonous, repeated 'zit-zit-zit' given in a distinctive bouncing display flight. One of the most widespread Old World passerines. Occurs around the Mediterranean and is expanding northwards.

children.name: Cisticola erlangeri
children.description: Endemic to the Ethiopian highlands. Similar to the Zitting Cisticola but darker and more richly coloured. Inhabits montane grassland and moorland above 2,000 metres.

children.name: Cisticola albiscutata
children.description: Found in eastern and southern Africa. Distinctive rufous wing panels and a heavily streaked back. Inhabits moist grassland and marshes. Its display flight is accompanied by a dry, insect-like buzzing.

children.name: Cisticola exilis
children.description: A tiny cisticola in which the breeding male develops a striking golden-yellow head and breast contrasting with a streaked brown back. Found from India through Southeast Asia to the Philippines, New Guinea, and eastern Australia. Its song is a buzzing trill given during a circling display flight above grassland and marshland.

children.name: Cisticola galactotes
children.description: A medium-sized cisticola of moist African grasslands and marshes, with a heavily streaked back and a warm rufous crown. Its song, a long, winding series of notes that rises and falls in pitch, gives it its common name. It is often seen perched on grass stems or doing a low, fluttering display flight.

children.name: Cisticola carruthersi
children.description: A swamp-dwelling cisticola found in papyrus marshes and wetlands of East Africa, from Uganda to Tanzania. It is dark and heavily streaked with a rufous crown and nape. Its song is a rapid, metallic trill. Named after the ornithologist who documented its distinctiveness from the Winding Cisticola.

children.name: Cisticola robustus
children.description: A large, robust cisticola of montane grasslands and moorlands across eastern and central Africa. Dark brown above with heavy streaking, a rufous crown, and a stout bill that distinguishes it from other highland cisticolas. Its song is a loud, far-carrying series of whistles and buzzes.

children.name: Cisticola cantans
children.description: A common cisticola of moist savanna and montane grasslands across sub-Saharan Africa. Warm brown above with dark streaking, pale below with a buff wash. Its song — a distinctive, repetitive three-note whistle — is one of the most characteristic sounds of African highland grasslands. It perches conspicuously on tall grass stems and low bushes.

children.name: Cisticola subruficapilla
children.description: Endemic to southern Africa, this small cisticola has a grey back, rufous crown, and pale underparts. It is common in fynbos, Karoo scrub, and arid grassland. Its song is a short, harsh buzzing, and it is often seen performing a low, fluttering display flight over open terrain.

children.name: Prinia
children.description: Prinias — long-tailed, active warblers of Africa and Asia, often called 'long-tailed warblers'. They hold their slender tails cocked at an angle and flick them characteristically. About 25 species, many of which have adapted well to human-modified habitats like farmland and gardens.

children.name: Prinia gracilis
children.description: A delicate, pale prinia with a very long, graduated tail. Buff-brown above, whitish below. Found across the Middle East, South Asia, and northeastern Africa. Inhabits dry scrub, tamarisk thickets, and reedbeds. Its tail is constantly in motion, wagging and fanning.

children.name: Prinia socialis
children.description: A common resident of the Indian subcontinent. Slaty-grey above in breeding plumage, warmer brown in winter. Chestnut wing panel. Inhabits scrub, grassland, and gardens. One of the few prinias with distinct breeding and non-breeding plumages.

children.name: Prinia subflava
children.description: The most widespread African prinia. Warm brown upperparts, buffy-white underparts, and a long cocked tail. Found across sub-Saharan Africa in grassland, scrub, and cultivation. Its song is a rapid, repetitive trill.

children.name: Prinia cinereocapilla
children.description: A striking prinia with a grey crown, warm brown upperparts, and whitish underparts. It is restricted to the Himalayan foothills from northern India to Nepal and Bhutan. Inhabits scrubby grasslands and forest edges, where its sharp, repeated calls give away its presence as it flits through tangled vegetation.

children.name: Prinia superciliaris
children.description: A large, long-tailed prinia of montane forests from the eastern Himalayas to Southeast Asia. Dark brown above with a prominent white supercilium, greyish underparts, and a very long graduated tail. It inhabits dense undergrowth in hill and montane forests, where its loud, chattering song carries far.

children.name: Prinia rufescens
children.description: A small, warm brown prinia with a rufous tint to the upperparts and a long, graduated tail. Ranges from the eastern Himalayas through Myanmar to Indochina and the Malay Peninsula. Inhabits scrub, grass, and forest clearings in lowland and hill areas. Its song is a series of high-pitched, insect-like notes.

children.name: Prinia inornata
children.description: A drab, plain-looking prinia with a long tail and unstreaked brown upperparts. Found across South and Southeast Asia from Pakistan and India to China, Myanmar, and Indonesia. Inhabits wet grasslands, reedbeds, and rice paddies. Its song is a monotonous, repeated trill that is a common sound in Asian wetlands.

children.name: Prinia sylvatica
children.description: A large, robust prinia of scrub and secondary forest in the Indian subcontinent. Rich brown above with a long, broad tail that is often held cocked. Its song is a loud, explosive series of notes. It is more secretive than other prinias, typically staying hidden in dense thickets.

children.name: Prinia flavicans
children.description: A striking prinia of southern Africa with a bold black breast band contrasting with white underparts and a pale grey-brown back. Found in arid savanna, scrub, and woodland edges. Its song is a rapid, high-pitched trill, and it is often seen perching conspicuously on top of bushes with its tail cocked vertically.

children.name: Prinia crinigera
children.description: A large prinia with heavily streaked upperparts, a white supercilium, and a very long, broad tail. Found from the Himalayas through Myanmar to southern China and Taiwan. Inhabits scrub, grassland, and forest edges at middle to high elevations. Its distinctive chattering song and habit of perching on exposed grass stems make it relatively easy to observe.

children.name: Prinia flaviventris
children.description: A small prinia with olive-green upperparts, a bright yellow belly, and a long graduated tail. Ranges from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo. Inhabits wet grasslands, reeds, and scrub near water. Its song is a monotonous, buzzy trill, and it actively flits through vegetation flicking its tail.

children.name: Prinia familiaris
children.description: A distinctive prinia endemic to Indonesia, found on Sumatra, Java, and Bali. It has grey upperparts, a long tail with white tips, and striking white wing bars that are visible in flight. It inhabits mangroves, coastal scrub, and gardens. Its song is a series of musical trills and whistles.

children.name: Hemitesia
children.description: A small genus of bush warblers from Africa, with a disjunct distribution in the highlands of East Africa and lowland forests of West Africa. They are skulking, short-tailed warblers that inhabit dense undergrowth and forest floor vegetation. Their songs are high-pitched and insect-like, and they are more often heard than seen.

children.name: Hemitesia pallidipes
children.description: A shy, subtly plumaged bush warbler found in the undergrowth of East African montane forests. It has olive-brown upperparts, pale grey underparts, and notably pale pinkish legs that give it its name. Its song is a thin, high-pitched trill that is difficult to locate, and it prefers dense thickets where it forages close to the ground.

children.name: Horornis
children.description: An Asian genus of bush warblers formerly included in Cettia. They are small, cryptically plumaged birds of dense undergrowth in forests and scrublands, with a distribution from the Himalayas through East and Southeast Asia. They are renowned for their loud, carrying songs that seem disproportionate to their size.

children.name: Horornis flavolivaceus
children.description: A plain olive-green bush warbler with a pale yellowish throat and belly, found from the Himalayas through Myanmar and southern China to northern Vietnam. Despite its common name, it is typical of the bush warblers in its skulking behaviour and loud, distinctive song — a ringing series of whistles. It inhabits dense scrub, bamboo thickets, and forest edges at middle elevations.
